title: Stable Angina
chief_complaint: Chest tightness when walking uphill for the past 3 months. It goes away when I rest. Today it happened while walking to the mailbox.

physical_exam.Cardiac: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops

hidden_data.ecg: Normal sinus rhythm, no ST-T changes. No Q waves. Left axis deviation.
hidden_data.cxr: Normal cardiac silhouette. Clear lung fields. No pleural effusion.
hidden_data.stress_test: Exercise stress test positive for inducible ischemia at 7 METs. 2mm ST depression in leads V4-V6 at peak exercise, resolving within 3 minutes of rest.

correct_diagnosis: Stable Angina Pectoris due to Coronary Artery Disease
key_learning_points: Stable angina is predictable chest pain triggered by exertion or stress, relieved by rest or nitroglycerin within minutes
Unstable angina is new-onset, crescendo, or occurring at rest - this is an ACS equivalent requiring urgent management
Exercise stress testing is the first-line diagnostic test for stable CAD in patients with intermediate pre-test probability
Lipid management with high-intensity statin is indicated for all patients with CAD regardless of baseline LDL
Anti-anginal therapy (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, nitrates) improves symptoms but does not reduce mortality

patient_responses.what brings you: I get this tightness in my chest when I walk uphill or when I'm stressed at work. It's like someone is sitting on my chest. It goes away after I sit down for a few minutes.
patient_responses.pain details: It's a pressure, not sharp. No radiation. It lasts maybe 3-5 minutes. Today it happened just walking to the mailbox which worried me.
patient_responses.associated symptoms: No shortness of breath, no nausea, no sweating. Just the chest pressure.
patient_responses.risk factors: I have high cholesterol, my father had a heart attack at 65. I sit at a desk all day. I used to smoke but quit 10 years ago.
patient_responses.medications: I take atorvastatin 20mg daily. That's it.

hints.history: Ask about character, duration, triggers, and relieving factors of chest pain. Quantify exercise tolerance (METs).
hints.examination: Look for signs of heart failure, valvular disease, and peripheral vascular disease.
hints.labs: ECG, troponin, lipid panel, CBC, CMP. Stress testing is key for diagnosis.
hints.imaging: CXR to rule out other causes. Stress echo adds sensitivity. Coronary CTA or angiography for definitive diagnosis.

feedback.correct_message: Excellent! You correctly recognized stable angina from the history - exertional chest pressure relieved by rest with normal ECG and troponin. This is classic stable CAD requiring risk stratification and medical optimization.
feedback.partially_correct_message: Good effort! The presentation of exertional chest pain with normal biomarkers is consistent with stable angina, not ACS. Remember: Unstable angina is defined by new-onset, crescendo pattern, or rest pain.
feedback.incorrect_message: This presentation is classic for stable angina - exertional chest pressure relieved by rest. The normal ECG and troponin rule out ACS. Stress testing is the appropriate next step for diagnosis and risk stratification.
clinical_pearls: Beta-blockers are first-line anti-anginal therapy and reduce mortality post-MI, but do NOT reduce mortality in stable CAD without prior MI
High-intensity statin (Atorvastatin 80mg) is indicated for ALL patients with CAD regardless of baseline LDL
Aspirin 81mg daily reduces cardiovascular events in secondary prevention

ecg.comment: ECG within 10 minutes of presentation is mandatory for all chest pain to rule out STEMI

troponin.comment: Rules out NSTEMI. Negative troponin at 0h and 3h rules out acute coronary syndrome

lipid_panel.comment: Identifies dyslipidemia requiring aggressive statin therapy

stress_test.comment: Diagnostic gold standard for stable CAD. Determines functional capacity and ischemia burden

cxr.comment: Helpful to rule out other causes of chest pain but not diagnostic for CAD

missing_tests: HbA1c - Screen for diabetes, a major CAD risk factor
feedback.attending_note: Doctor, you managed this case well. Remember that stable angina is a clinical diagnosis based on history. The key is distinguishing it from unstable angina which requires urgent hospitalization. Focus on aggressive risk factor modification - statin, aspirin, blood pressure control, and lifestyle changes. Refer for stress testing and cardiology follow-up.
